terms.etiology_clue: A localized developmental anomaly affecting enamel, dentin, and pulp in a regional distribution along a nerve pathway.
terms.management_clue: Affected teeth are typically extracted due to poor structural integrity, followed by prosthetic rehabilitation.

terms.etiology_clue: A rare unilateral condition involving abnormal development of teeth, bone, and soft tissue within a maxillary segment.
terms.management_clue: Management is conservative and supportive, focusing on prosthetic replacement of missing or malformed teeth.

terms.etiology_clue: Enamel hypoplasia of a permanent tooth caused by periapical infection or trauma to its overlying primary predecessor.
terms.management_clue: Cosmetic restoration with composite resin or a crown addresses the localized enamel defect on the affected tooth.

terms.etiology_clue: Failure of thyroid gland descent during embryogenesis results in ectopic thyroid tissue at the foramen cecum.
terms.management_clue: Ablation with radioactive iodine or surgical excision is indicated when the ectopic tissue causes airway obstruction or dysphagia.

terms.etiology_clue: Entrapment of ectodermal elements along embryonic fusion lines produces a cyst lined by keratinizing epithelium with skin appendages.
terms.management_clue: Complete surgical enucleation is curative, with the approach determined by whether the lesion is above or below the mylohyoid.

terms.etiology_clue: Congenital absence of the submandibular gland, often associated with other salivary gland or craniofacial developmental anomalies.
terms.management_clue: No treatment is required unless xerostomia is symptomatic, in which case salivary substitutes are prescribed.

terms.etiology_clue: Anomalous budding of the primitive foregut during embryogenesis gives rise to a cyst lined by gastrointestinal or respiratory epithelium.
terms.management_clue: Surgical excision is the treatment of choice to prevent infection, hemorrhage, or compressive complications.

terms.etiology_clue: Extravasation or retention of mucus from the sublingual gland produces a fluctuant, bluish, dome-shaped swelling in the floor of mouth.
terms.management_clue: Marsupialization or excision of the sublingual gland is preferred to reduce recurrence compared to simple drainage alone.

terms.etiology_clue: Obstructive sialolithiasis or ascending bacterial infection causes painful, recurrent swelling of the submandibular gland at mealtime.
terms.management_clue: Stone removal via sialendoscopy or open surgery combined with antibiotics resolves the obstructive inflammatory episode.

terms.etiology_clue: Traumatic disruption of the submandibular gland capsule or duct allows saliva to accumulate within adjacent connective tissue.
terms.management_clue: Repeated aspiration, pressure dressings, or botulinum toxin injection reduces salivary flow until the defect resolves spontaneously.

terms.etiology_clue: Mucous extravasation from minor salivary gland duct disruption in the submandibular region forms a superficial soft tissue swelling.
terms.management_clue: Excision of the mucocele together with the causative minor gland is required to prevent recurrence.

terms.etiology_clue: A pleomorphic adenoma arising in the sublingual gland presents as a slow-growing, painless, firm mass in the floor of mouth.
terms.management_clue: Wide local excision with an intact capsule is essential to minimize the risk of recurrence or malignant transformation.

terms.etiology_clue: The sublingual gland has the highest malignancy rate among major salivary glands, most commonly presenting as adenoid cystic carcinoma.
terms.management_clue: Radical surgical resection with adjuvant radiotherapy is standard due to perineural invasion propensity and high recurrence rates.

terms.etiology_clue: Clonal proliferation of B or T lymphocytes within cervical lymph nodes produces firm, non-tender, rapidly enlarging nodal masses.
terms.management_clue: Combination chemotherapy regimens such as R-CHOP, with or without radiotherapy, are the cornerstone of systemic treatment.
